title: Engage Qualified Legal Counsel for Token Classification
description: Before any token design, development, or marketing activity begins, engage legal counsel with specific expertise in securities law and digital asset regulation in every jurisdiction where the token will be offered or traded. Legal counsel must be qualified to provide opinions under US federal securities law (for US offerings), EU MiCA regulation (for EU offerings), and the securities and payment services laws of each target jurisdiction. The engagement should cover a comprehensive analysis of the token's legal classification under each applicable regime, identification of registration or exemption requirements, assessment of AML/CFT obligations triggered by the token offering, review of marketing and advertising restrictions, identification of sanctions and export control implications, and advice on secondary market trading considerations. Do not rely on a single jurisdiction's analysis — a token classified as a utility token in one jurisdiction may be classified as a security or asset-referenced token in another. Engage local counsel in each material target jurisdiction. Retain all legal opinions and make them available for regulatory examination. Do not proceed with token design or development until the initial legal framework assessment is complete.
threshold_triggers: Decision to create or issue any digital token
Before any public announcement of token plans
documents_required: Engagement letter with qualified securities counsel
Token concept description for legal review
systems: Document management system for legal opinions
regulatory_basis: All jurisdictions: prerequisite to compliance with applicable securities and token offering regulations
risk_indicators: Proceeding without legal counsel
Using counsel without digital asset expertise
Relying on legal analysis from a single jurisdiction
Legal opinion obtained after token distribution has begun

title: Howey Test Self-Assessment (US Securities Classification)
description: Conduct a thorough self-assessment of the token against the four prongs of the Howey Test and the SEC's 2019 Framework for Investment Contract Analysis of Digital Assets, regardless of whether US investors are being targeted (because US persons may access the token through secondary markets). Prong 1 — Investment of Money: document whether purchasers exchange fiat currency, other crypto-assets, or any other form of value to acquire the token. Prong 2 — Common Enterprise: assess whether proceeds from the token sale are pooled and used collectively, and whether investors' fortunes are linked to each other or to the project team's efforts. Prong 3 — Reasonable Expectation of Profits: evaluate whether marketing materials, social media, or public statements create an expectation of price appreciation; whether the token is marketed for consumptive use or investment; whether the token is listed on secondary markets; and whether supply constraints or burn mechanisms suggest appreciation. Prong 4 — Efforts of Others: assess whether an Active Participant (AP) controls development, governance, or operations; whether the network is sufficiently decentralised; and whether token holders rely on the AP for value creation. Document the analysis for each prong with specific evidence. Where any prong is unclear, assume the SEC would find it satisfied. If all four prongs are met, treat the token as a security for US purposes and pursue registration or an exemption.
threshold_triggers: All token offerings regardless of whether US is a target market
Token design changes that may affect classification
Annual re-assessment for ongoing distributions
documents_required: Evidence supporting analysis for each prong
Marketing materials reviewed for profit expectation language
regulatory_basis: SEC v. W.J. Howey Co., 328 U.S. 293 (1946); SEC Framework for Investment Contract Analysis of Digital Assets (April 2019); SEC v. Ripple Labs (2023)
risk_indicators: Token has no functional utility at launch
Marketing emphasises investment returns or price appreciation
Team retains large allocation suggesting ongoing influence
Token is immediately listed on secondary markets
Project roadmap depends entirely on the core team's development efforts
Token sale proceeds fund development (not operations of existing platform)

title: MiCA Token Classification Assessment (EU)
description: Conduct a classification assessment under MiCA (Regulation EU 2023/1114) to determine which MiCA category the token falls into, as this determines the applicable regulatory requirements. MiCA establishes three categories with different regulatory regimes. Asset-Referenced Token (ART): a crypto-asset that purports to maintain a stable value by referencing another value or right or a combination thereof, including one or more official currencies (but not a single currency — that would be an EMT). Assess whether the token references a basket of currencies, commodities, or other assets. If classified as an ART, the issuer must obtain authorisation under Article 16, prepare an ART-specific white paper under Article 19, maintain reserve assets under Article 36, and meet own funds requirements under Article 22. E-Money Token (EMT): a crypto-asset that references exactly one single official currency. If classified as an EMT, the issuer must be authorised as a credit institution or e-money institution and comply with Articles 48-58. Other Crypto-Asset: any crypto-asset that is not an ART or EMT and is not a financial instrument under MiFID II. This includes utility tokens and exchange tokens. For this category, the issuer must prepare and publish a white paper under Article 6 and comply with marketing requirements under Article 7. Document the classification analysis with specific reference to MiCA definitions and seek legal counsel confirmation.
threshold_triggers: Any token offering to EU persons
Token listed on EU-licensed CASP
Token classification may have changed due to design modifications
documents_required: Token characteristics mapping to MiCA definitions
Comparison with MiFID II specified instruments (to confirm MiCA applies)
systems: Legal analysis template for MiCA classification
regulatory_basis: MiCA: Article 3(1)(5)-(7) definitions; Article 4 (other crypto-assets); Article 16 (ARTs); Article 48 (EMTs)
risk_indicators: Token has characteristics of multiple MiCA categories
Token references a single currency but also other assets (ART vs EMT boundary)
Token may qualify as a financial instrument under MiFID II (outside MiCA scope)
Classification analysis not reviewed by EU-qualified legal counsel

title: Multi-Jurisdiction Classification Matrix
description: Create a comprehensive classification matrix mapping the token's regulatory status across all target and material jurisdictions. For each jurisdiction, document the token's classification (security, payment token, utility token, e-money, ART, EMT, commodity, DPT, etc.), the applicable regulatory regime (Securities Act, MiCA, PS Act, FSMA, etc.), whether registration, authorisation, or licensing is required, available exemptions, the regulatory authority with oversight (SEC, ESMA, FCA, MAS, VARA, etc.), and any specific restrictions or prohibitions. Key jurisdictions to assess even if not directly targeted include the US (due to global reach of SEC enforcement), EU (due to MiCA's broad scope and passporting), UK (if any UK persons may access the token), Singapore, UAE, and any jurisdiction where the token will be listed on an exchange. The classification matrix should be reviewed and updated whenever the token's characteristics change (e.g., new features, governance changes, new use cases) and whenever relevant regulations change. This matrix serves as the master reference document for the token's compliance strategy and should be maintained by the legal and compliance team throughout the token's lifecycle.
threshold_triggers: Initial token design finalisation
Expansion to new jurisdictions
Token feature or governance changes
Regulatory changes in any assessed jurisdiction
documents_required: Supporting legal opinions for each jurisdiction
Regulatory regime summary for each jurisdiction
regulatory_basis: All applicable securities, payment services, and crypto-asset regulations across target jurisdictions
risk_indicators: Token classified differently across jurisdictions without mitigation strategy
Missing analysis for jurisdictions where token is accessible
Classification matrix not updated after token design changes
No local counsel engaged for material jurisdictions

title: White Paper / Offering Document Preparation
description: Prepare the required disclosure document — a crypto-asset white paper under MiCA, an offering memorandum or prospectus for securities offerings, or equivalent documents for other jurisdictions. For MiCA white papers (other crypto-assets under Article 6), include: information about the issuer (legal name, registered office, LEI, date of registration), description of the crypto-asset project including objectives and roadmap, description of the crypto-asset (type, consensus mechanism, protocol), rights and obligations attached to the token, underlying technology and security features, risks relating to the crypto-asset and the issuer and the project, and a summary in plain non-technical language. For MiCA ART white papers (Article 19), additional sections on reserve asset composition, custody, and stabilisation mechanism are required. For US securities offerings (Reg D, Reg A+, Reg S), prepare the appropriate offering memorandum or Form 1-A with required disclosures including financial statements, risk factors, use of proceeds, management information, and legal opinions. Ensure all disclosure documents are fair, clear, and not misleading. Have all documents reviewed by qualified legal counsel. File or notify the document with the relevant competent authority as required. Publish the white paper on the issuer's website before the offering commences.
threshold_triggers: Decision to offer token to the public
Decision to seek admission to trading on a platform
MiCA: before offering or admission to trading
US: before first sale of securities
systems: Regulatory filing systems (SEC EDGAR, ESMA register)
regulatory_basis: EU: MiCA Art. 4-9 (white paper); US: Securities Act Section 5, Reg D, Reg A+; UK: FSMA prospectus requirements; SG: SFA Part XIII; AE: VARA issuance rules
risk_indicators: White paper contains exaggerated or unsubstantiated claims
Material risks omitted from disclosure
Financial statements not audited (where required)
White paper not reviewed by legal counsel
White paper published after offering has commenced

title: Jurisdiction Blacklist and Geo-Blocking Implementation
description: Establish a list of jurisdictions from which token purchasers will be blocked, and implement technical measures to enforce the restrictions. At minimum, block all jurisdictions subject to comprehensive sanctions by major sanctioning authorities — Iran, North Korea, Syria, Cuba, and the Crimea, Donetsk, and Luhansk regions of Ukraine. Additionally, block jurisdictions where the token offering would violate local law and the issuer has not obtained the required authorisation or exemption. For most token offerings, this includes the United States (unless a Reg D, Reg A+, or Reg S exemption is being relied upon), the EU (unless MiCA white paper requirements are met), the UK (unless FCA financial promotions requirements are met), and any other jurisdiction where the token may constitute a regulated product without the issuer having the required licence. Implement geo-blocking through IP address filtering, VPN detection, ID verification country checks, declaration of non-restricted jurisdiction status, and blocking of restricted country phone numbers and payment methods. Geo-blocking must be implemented at the token sale platform, the issuer's website, and any marketing channels. Document the jurisdiction blacklist with the legal rationale for each blocked jurisdiction. Update the blacklist as regulations change. Be aware that geo-blocking alone is not sufficient to establish a Reg S safe harbour — the SEC considers whether directed selling efforts have been made in the US regardless of technical blocking.
threshold_triggers: Before any token offering commences
Before marketing materials are published
Regulatory changes in any jurisdiction
Sanctions list updates
regulatory_basis: US: Reg S (offshore safe harbour); OFAC: comprehensive sanctions; EU: MiCA (EU establishment requirement); UK: Section 21 FSMA; Each jurisdiction's local restrictions
risk_indicators: Geo-blocking easily circumvented by VPN
No VPN detection implemented
Jurisdiction blacklist not updated after sanctions changes
Marketing materials accessible from blocked jurisdictions
No user declaration of jurisdiction collected
Blocked jurisdiction residents found among token holders

title: Investor Accreditation Verification (US Reg D)
description: If the token offering relies on Regulation D exemptions for US investors, implement a robust process to verify investor accreditation status. Under Rule 506(b), the issuer can sell to an unlimited number of accredited investors and up to 35 sophisticated non-accredited investors, with no general solicitation. Under Rule 506(c), the issuer can use general solicitation but must take reasonable steps to verify that all purchasers are accredited investors. Accredited investor verification methods under Rule 506(c) include: for income-based qualification ($200K individual/$300K joint for two most recent years), review of tax returns, W-2 forms, or other IRS documents and obtain a written representation that the investor reasonably expects the requisite income in the current year; for net worth-based qualification ($1M excluding primary residence), review of bank statements, brokerage statements, tax assessments, and credit reports; or obtain a verification letter from a registered broker-dealer, SEC-registered investment adviser, licensed attorney, or CPA confirming accredited investor status within the prior three months. Collect and retain all accreditation documentation. Re-verify accreditation for ongoing or subsequent offerings — prior verification does not carry forward indefinitely. Maintain a current investor register documenting each investor's accreditation status, verification method, and documentation reviewed.
threshold_triggers: Token offering relying on Reg D 506(b) or 506(c)
Each new US investor onboarding
Re-verification for subsequent token offerings
documents_required: Income documentation (tax returns, W-2s) or net worth documentation (bank/brokerage statements)
regulatory_basis: US: Regulation D Rules 501, 506(b), 506(c); SEC guidance on accredited investor verification
risk_indicators: Self-certification used as sole verification method under Rule 506(c)
Income or net worth documentation appears altered
Investor provides inconsistent financial information
Large number of investors claiming accredited status (statistical anomaly)
No re-verification for subsequent offerings

title: Regulatory Filing Preparation (Form D, Form 1-A, MiCA Notification)
description: Prepare and file all required regulatory notices and forms before commencing the token offering. For US Reg D offerings, file Form D with the SEC electronically through EDGAR within 15 calendar days after the first sale of securities. Form D requires disclosure of the issuer's name and address, the type of exemption claimed (Rule 506(b) or 506(c)), the amount of securities offered and sold, and information about the issuer's directors and executive officers. Additionally, file Form D or equivalent notices with each US state in which the securities are sold or offered. For US Reg A+ offerings, file Form 1-A on EDGAR and wait for SEC qualification before commencing sales. For EU token offerings under MiCA, notify the crypto-asset white paper to the competent authority of the home Member State at least 20 working days before the publication date. For MiCA ART issuance, submit a full authorisation application under Article 17. For UK offerings of security tokens, comply with prospectus requirements under FSMA or obtain an exemption. For Singapore offerings of security tokens, file a prospectus with MAS under the SFA or rely on an exemption. Maintain copies of all filings and regulatory correspondence.
threshold_triggers: US Reg D: within 15 days of first sale
US Reg A+: before commencing sales (qualification required)
EU MiCA: at least 20 working days before publication
Each jurisdiction's specific filing requirements
documents_required: Form 1-A and offering circular (US Reg A+)
regulatory_basis: US: Form D (Rule 503); Form 1-A (Reg A+); EU: MiCA Art. 8 (notification); UK: FSMA prospectus requirements; SG: SFA Part XIII
risk_indicators: Form D not filed within 15-day deadline
Commencing sales before Reg A+ qualification
MiCA white paper not notified before publication
State notice filings missed for states where securities were sold
Filing contains inaccurate or incomplete information

title: Token Economic Design Compliance Review
description: Conduct a compliance review of the token's economic design (tokenomics) to identify features that may create regulatory risk or trigger specific regulatory requirements. Review the following elements: total supply and whether it is fixed or inflationary/deflationary — burn mechanisms or supply caps that create scarcity may support an expectation of price appreciation (Howey Prong 3 indicator). Token allocation — large allocations to the founding team, early investors, or insiders with vesting schedules suggest the team has strong financial incentives to increase token value (Howey Prong 4 indicator). Staking mechanisms — if the token offers yield through staking, assess whether the staking is native on-chain validation (lower regulatory risk) or intermediated staking-as-a-service (higher regulatory risk per Kraken enforcement). Governance rights — assess whether governance token holders have meaningful governance power or whether governance is concentrated among insiders. Revenue distribution — any token that distributes revenue, fees, or profits to holders is likely a security. Buyback and burn programs funded by protocol revenue create economic equivalence to profit distribution. Ensure the tokenomics design is reviewed by legal counsel for each target jurisdiction. Document compliance-informed modifications to the tokenomics design.
threshold_triggers: Token economic design finalisation
Any material change to tokenomics
Before legal opinion is obtained
documents_required: Compliance review analysis for each tokenomic feature
Modified tokenomics (if changes made for compliance reasons)
regulatory_basis: US: SEC Framework (profit expectation and efforts of others analysis); EU: MiCA classification criteria; FATF: VASP definition
risk_indicators: Token has burn mechanism creating deflationary pressure
Team allocation exceeds 20% with vesting
Token distributes protocol revenue to holders
Staking yields are set by the team rather than determined by protocol
Governance is concentrated among founders despite decentralisation claims
Buyback programs funded by protocol revenue

title: Marketing and Advertising Compliance Review
description: Review all marketing materials, advertising content, social media posts, press releases, and public communications related to the token offering for compliance with applicable regulations before publication. For US offerings, ensure marketing does not constitute general solicitation if relying on Reg D 506(b), or that all purchasers reached through general solicitation under 506(c) are verified accredited investors. Avoid language suggesting guaranteed returns, comparing tokens to traditional securities or investments, or making price predictions. For EU/MiCA, ensure marketing communications are clearly identifiable as such, are fair, clear, and not misleading, are consistent with the white paper, and include a statement that the white paper has been published with its URL. For UK, ensure compliance with the financial promotions regime — all promotions must be approved by an FCA-authorised person or made by an FCA-registered firm, must include prescribed risk warnings, and must not be misleading. For Singapore, ensure compliance with MAS PS-G02 restrictions — no advertising to the general public, no engagement of social media influencers, no incentives to trade. For UAE/VARA, ensure compliance with the Marketing Rulebook including risk warnings and compliance pre-approval. Maintain a record of all marketing materials and their approval status.
threshold_triggers: Before any marketing material is published
Before any public announcement
Before social media campaigns launch
Before press releases are issued
documents_required: Marketing materials with compliance review annotations
Compliance sign-off on each piece of content
systems: Content management system with compliance workflow
regulatory_basis: US: Reg D general solicitation rules; EU: MiCA Art. 7; UK: FSMA s.21, PS23/6; SG: MAS PS-G02; AE: VARA Marketing Rulebook
risk_indicators: Marketing materials promise or imply guaranteed returns
Price predictions or price targets included in marketing
Marketing disseminated before white paper publication
Social media influencers promoting token without required disclosures
Marketing accessible in jurisdictions where offering is not authorised
Risk warnings missing or insufficiently prominent

title: Smart Contract Audit and Security Assessment
description: Before deploying any token smart contract, conduct a comprehensive security audit by a qualified independent smart contract auditing firm. The audit must cover the token smart contract code, any associated contracts (vesting, governance, staking, liquidity), and the deployment process. The audit should identify critical vulnerabilities including reentrancy attacks, integer overflow/underflow, access control weaknesses, front-running vulnerabilities, logic errors, and privilege escalation risks. Additionally, assess whether the smart contract contains any backdoor functions, owner override capabilities, or hidden minting functions that could undermine the token's stated properties. Review the smart contract for compliance-relevant features: does the contract support regulatory functions such as transfer restrictions (for restricted securities), blacklisting (for sanctions compliance), and pausing (for emergency situations)? Does the contract accurately implement the tokenomics described in the white paper? Engage at least one reputable auditing firm (e.g., Trail of Bits, OpenZeppelin, Consensys Diligence, Certik, Halborn). Publish the audit report or a summary for investor transparency. Remediate all critical and high-severity findings before deployment. Consider implementing a bug bounty programme post-deployment for ongoing security.
threshold_triggers: Before smart contract deployment to mainnet
After any material smart contract upgrade
Before tokens are made available to the public
documents_required: Smart contract audit report from independent auditor
regulatory_basis: EU: MiCA Art. 6 (technology disclosure); AE: VARA Technology Governance Rulebook; SG: MAS TRM Guidelines; Best practice across all jurisdictions
risk_indicators: No independent audit conducted before deployment
Critical vulnerabilities identified but not remediated
Audit firm lacks credible track record
Hidden owner functions not disclosed in white paper
No bug bounty or ongoing security programme
Token contract allows unlimited minting by owner

title: AML/KYC Implementation for Token Sale Participants
description: Implement AML/KYC procedures for all token sale participants proportionate to the offering type, jurisdiction, and risk profile. For public token offerings, implement the full KYC/AML onboarding process as detailed in the KYC SOP, including identity verification, sanctions screening, PEP screening, adverse media screening, and source of funds verification. For private placements to accredited/qualified investors, implement KYC/AML alongside investor accreditation verification. Screen all participants against applicable sanctions lists (OFAC SDN, EU, UN, UK OFSI) before accepting funds and before distributing tokens. Implement ongoing monitoring of participants during the offering period. For MiCA-compliant offerings, ensure compliance with AMLD requirements including CDD for all participants. For VARA-compliant offerings, implement VARA AML/CFT Rulebook requirements. Process all participant payments through compliant payment channels — do not accept anonymous crypto deposits without conducting blockchain analysis on the source wallets. Maintain a complete register of all participants including identity information, amounts contributed, tokens allocated, and KYC/AML documentation. Reject participants who fail KYC/AML checks and return their funds through the original payment channel.
threshold_triggers: All token sale participants regardless of amount
Before accepting any funds from participants
Before distributing any tokens to participants
documents_required: Source of funds documentation (proportionate to risk)
systems: Token sale platform with integrated KYC
regulatory_basis: US: BSA/AML requirements; EU: AMLD5 + MiCA; UK: MLR 2017; SG: MAS PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Participant identity cannot be verified
Participant is resident in blocked jurisdiction
Participant's payment source has high-risk blockchain exposure
Participant uses multiple identities or accounts
Large contribution from newly established entity

title: Token Lockup and Vesting Schedule Implementation
description: Implement token lockup periods and vesting schedules as required by applicable regulations and as described in the white paper or offering documents. For US Reg D offerings, tokens are restricted securities subject to resale limitations under Rule 144 — implement a minimum one-year holding period (for non-reporting issuers) during which tokens cannot be transferred on secondary markets. Implement technical enforcement through smart contract transfer restrictions that prevent restricted token transfers during the lockup period. For team and insider allocations, implement vesting schedules that typically include a cliff period (commonly 6-12 months) followed by monthly or quarterly vesting over 2-4 years. Ensure vesting schedules are enforced through smart contract time-locks rather than relying on voluntary compliance. For EU MiCA offerings, comply with any applicable lockup requirements and ensure the white paper accurately describes the vesting schedule and transfer restrictions. Document all lockup and vesting parameters including start dates, cliff periods, vesting schedules, and any acceleration triggers. Publish lockup and vesting information in the white paper and on the issuer's website. Implement monitoring to detect and prevent circumvention of lockup restrictions (e.g., through derivative instruments or side agreements that effectively transfer economic interest during the lockup period).
threshold_triggers: Token generation event
Distribution of tokens to team, advisors, and investors
Reg D restricted securities distribution
Any token allocation with vesting conditions
documents_required: Vesting schedule published in white paper
systems: Token management platform (Carta for token cap tables)
regulatory_basis: US: Rule 144 (restricted securities resale); EU: MiCA white paper disclosure requirements; Best practice for all jurisdictions
risk_indicators: No transfer restrictions implemented for restricted securities
Vesting schedule not enforced through smart contracts
Team members selling tokens before vesting cliff
Side agreements that effectively circumvent lockup
Lockup/vesting terms not disclosed in offering documents

title: Secondary Market Trading Restrictions and Exchange Listing Compliance
description: Implement appropriate controls for secondary market trading of the token, including restrictions required by securities regulations and coordination with exchange listing processes. For tokens classified as securities in any jurisdiction, implement transfer restrictions that prevent transfers to persons in jurisdictions where the token has not been registered or exempted. For Reg D restricted securities, implement Rule 144 compliance procedures for any resale, including verification of holding period satisfaction, current public information availability (for affiliate sales), and volume limitations (for affiliate sales). For Reg S offshore tokens, implement distribution compliance period restrictions preventing flow-back to US persons. When listing on exchanges, coordinate with the exchange's listing team to ensure the token meets the exchange's listing requirements, that the exchange is appropriately licensed in the jurisdictions where it will offer the token, and that the exchange will enforce any trading restrictions required by the token's regulatory status. Under MiCA, trading platform operators have obligations to assess whether a token complies with their listing rules and whether a white paper has been published. Provide the exchange with all necessary documentation including legal opinions, white paper, and any transfer restriction specifications.
threshold_triggers: Before listing on any exchange or trading platform
Before enabling secondary market transfers
Expiry of lockup or distribution compliance periods
documents_required: Legal opinion on token classification for exchange's jurisdiction
MiCA white paper for exchange listing (EU)
regulatory_basis: US: Securities Act Section 5, Rule 144, Reg S; EU: MiCA Art. 68 (trading platform listing obligations); UK: prospectus/admission requirements; SG: SFA; AE: VARA Exchange Services Rulebook
risk_indicators: Token listed on exchange without transfer restrictions for restricted jurisdictions
Restricted securities trading before holding period expiry
Exchange not licensed in jurisdiction where it offers the token
No coordination with exchange on regulatory requirements
Token accessible to investors in blocked jurisdictions through exchange

title: Ongoing Disclosure and Communication Obligations
description: Implement ongoing disclosure and communication obligations as required by applicable regulations for as long as the token is held by the public or traded on secondary markets. Under MiCA Article 12, issuers of crypto-assets must maintain a website providing clear, accurate, and up-to-date information about the token, the issuer, and the project. The issuer must inform holders of any material change that may affect the value of or rights attached to the token. Under MiCA, the white paper must be updated and republished when material changes occur, with notification to the competent authority. For US securities, ongoing reporting obligations depend on the registration status — Reg A+ Tier 2 issuers must file annual reports on Form 1-K, semi-annual reports on Form 1-SA, and current event reports on Form 1-U. Reg D issuers generally do not have SEC reporting obligations but must provide information to investors as required by the exemption and applicable state laws. For tokens listed on exchanges, comply with the exchange's ongoing listing requirements including disclosure of material events, financial reporting, and governance updates. Establish a process for promptly disclosing material events including security breaches, smart contract vulnerabilities, team changes, regulatory developments, and financial difficulties. Maintain a communications log documenting all public disclosures.
threshold_triggers: Token launch and ongoing
Material events requiring disclosure
Periodic reporting deadlines
White paper updates needed
regulatory_basis: EU: MiCA Art. 12 (ongoing obligations); US: Reg A+ reporting (Form 1-K, 1-SA, 1-U); Exchange listing rules; UK: ongoing obligations under FSMA
risk_indicators: Material event not disclosed to holders in a timely manner
White paper not updated to reflect material changes
Website contains outdated or inaccurate information
Periodic reports not filed on time
No communication protocol for security incidents

title: Token Holder Right of Withdrawal Management (MiCA)
description: Implement the right of withdrawal for retail holders as required under MiCA Article 10 for crypto-assets other than ARTs and EMTs. Where tokens are offered directly by the issuer (or by a third party acting on the issuer's behalf), retail holders who purchase directly from the issuer must have a right to withdraw their purchase agreement within 14 calendar days without giving any reason and without incurring any cost. Implement a clear and accessible withdrawal mechanism — provide a withdrawal form on the issuer's website and through the platform where the token was purchased. Inform prospective purchasers about the right of withdrawal in the crypto-asset white paper before they make their purchase decision. Process withdrawal requests within the 14-day cooling-off period by refunding the full amount paid by the retail holder without undue delay and in any event within 14 days of receipt of the withdrawal notice. The refund must be in the same payment method used for the original purchase unless the holder expressly agrees otherwise. The right of withdrawal does not apply where the token has been admitted to trading on a trading platform prior to the purchase (because the holder can sell on the secondary market at market price). Maintain records of all withdrawal requests and their processing.
threshold_triggers: All direct-to-retail token sales under MiCA
Withdrawal request received within 14 days of purchase
documents_required: Withdrawal right disclosure in white paper
regulatory_basis: EU: MiCA Article 10
risk_indicators: No withdrawal mechanism implemented for direct retail sales
Withdrawal right not disclosed in white paper
Refunds not processed within 14-day period
Fees charged for withdrawal exercise
Withdrawal mechanism inaccessible or overly complex

title: Regulatory Change Monitoring and Compliance Adaptation
description: Establish an ongoing process to monitor regulatory developments across all jurisdictions in which the token is offered or traded, and adapt compliance measures as regulations change. Assign responsibility for regulatory monitoring to the legal and compliance team. Subscribe to regulatory alert services and monitor official publications from the SEC, ESMA, FCA, MAS, VARA, FATF, and other relevant authorities. Participate in industry associations (Chamber of Digital Commerce, Global Digital Finance, Crypto Council for Innovation) to stay informed of upcoming regulatory changes and industry best practices. When a material regulatory change is identified, assess the impact on the token's classification, the issuer's obligations, and the firm's compliance measures. Update the multi-jurisdiction classification matrix (tok-004) to reflect the change. Modify compliance procedures, marketing materials, and technical controls as needed. Brief senior management and the board on material regulatory developments and their implications. Where a regulatory change fundamentally affects the token's viability in a jurisdiction (e.g., a new prohibition), assess whether the token offering must be withdrawn from that jurisdiction and implement appropriate measures. Document all regulatory changes monitored, impact assessments conducted, and compliance adaptations implemented.
threshold_triggers: Ongoing monitoring — continuous
FATF plenary meetings and mutual evaluation publications
Legislative enactments in any target jurisdiction
Regulatory guidance or enforcement action with implications
documents_required: Impact assessment for each material change
regulatory_basis: All jurisdictions: implied by the obligation to maintain ongoing compliance
risk_indicators: No systematic regulatory monitoring process
Material regulatory changes not identified until enforcement action
Classification matrix not updated to reflect regulatory changes
Compliance procedures lag behind regulatory requirements
No board briefing on significant regulatory developments

title: Tax Structuring and Reporting Compliance
description: Assess and implement tax compliance obligations for the token issuer and provide appropriate tax disclosures to token purchasers. Engage qualified tax counsel to advise on the tax implications of the token issuance in each relevant jurisdiction. Key tax considerations include: characterisation of the token offering proceeds (capital contribution, revenue, loan proceeds, etc.) and the applicable tax treatment for the issuer; withholding tax obligations on any distributions, yields, or staking rewards paid to token holders; VAT/GST treatment of the token sale (in many jurisdictions, utility token sales may be subject to VAT while security token sales may be exempt as financial services); transfer pricing implications for multi-jurisdiction issuance structures; permanent establishment risk created by token sale activities in foreign jurisdictions; and ongoing reporting obligations (US Form 1099-DA reporting by brokers from 2025, OECD Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework). Inform token purchasers in the white paper or offering document about their own tax obligations, including that they may be required to report capital gains, that yields or staking rewards may constitute taxable income, and that they should consult their own tax advisers. Implement tax information collection (W-8/W-9 forms for US tax reporting) where the issuer has broker reporting obligations.
threshold_triggers: Token economic design finalisation
Before token sale commences
When implementing staking or yield features
Annual tax reporting obligations
documents_required: Tax counsel opinion on token characterisation
regulatory_basis: US: IRS Notice 2014-21, Form 1099-DA requirements; EU: VAT Directive, DAC8; UK: HMRC crypto tax guidance; SG: IRAS guidance; OECD: CARF
risk_indicators: No tax counsel engaged before token issuance
VAT/GST treatment not assessed
No tax disclosure in white paper
Withholding obligations not identified or implemented
Tax information not collected from investors where required

title: Complaint Handling and Dispute Resolution
description: Establish and maintain an effective complaint handling and dispute resolution mechanism for token holders. Under MiCA Article 12, issuers must have effective arrangements for the prompt handling and proper investigation of complaints from holders. The complaint handling procedure must be published on the issuer's website and be available to holders free of charge. Implement a process that acknowledges receipt of complaints within 48 hours (VARA requirement, best practice for all jurisdictions), investigates complaints fairly and thoroughly, communicates the outcome of the investigation to the complainant within a reasonable timeframe (15 business days under VARA, 8 weeks for FCA-registered firms), and maintains records of all complaints and their resolution. Establish an escalation path for unresolved complaints, including information about external dispute resolution mechanisms where available. For EU issuers, inform complainants about the right to refer disputes to the competent authority or national ADR body. For UK firms, inform complainants about the Financial Ombudsman Service (though its jurisdiction over unregulated crypto is limited). Analyse complaint trends to identify systemic issues and implement corrective measures. Report complaint volumes and themes to senior management at least quarterly.
threshold_triggers: Receipt of any complaint from a token holder
Establishment of token offering (preventive implementation)
regulatory_basis: EU: MiCA Art. 12(2); UK: FCA DISP rules; SG: MAS conduct requirements; AE: VARA Consumer Protection Rulebook (48hr acknowledgement, 15-day resolution)
risk_indicators: No complaint handling procedure published
Complaints not acknowledged within required timeframe
High volume of unresolved complaints
Complaint trends indicating systemic product issues
No escalation path for unresolved complaints

title: Annual Compliance Review and Re-Assessment
description: Conduct a comprehensive annual compliance review of the token offering and all associated activities to ensure ongoing compliance with all applicable regulations. The annual review should cover a re-assessment of the token's classification in each jurisdiction (classifications can change as the project matures, decentralises, or the regulatory landscape evolves), verification that the white paper and all public disclosures remain accurate and up-to-date, review of marketing materials for continued compliance with applicable promotion rules, assessment of whether any new jurisdictions have been reached that require additional analysis or filings, review of AML/KYC procedures and their effectiveness for token offering participants, review of lockup and vesting schedule compliance (are transfer restrictions still in place where required?), assessment of secondary market trading compliance (are restricted securities being traded in violation of regulations?), review of ongoing disclosure obligations and their fulfilment, assessment of regulatory changes during the review period and their impact, and evaluation of any enforcement actions in the crypto industry that create new precedent or risk for the project. Produce an annual compliance report summarising findings, identified risks, and recommended actions. Present the report to the board or senior management and implement remediation actions for any identified deficiencies. Retain the annual review documentation as part of the firm's compliance records.
threshold_triggers: Annual anniversary of token launch
Material change in token or project
Significant regulatory development
regulatory_basis: All jurisdictions: ongoing compliance obligation; EU: MiCA Art. 12 (ongoing obligations); US: Reg A+ ongoing reporting
risk_indicators: No annual review conducted
Token classification not re-assessed despite project evolution
White paper not updated to reflect material changes
Marketing materials contain outdated or non-compliant content
Transfer restrictions expired without assessment of ongoing requirements
No board-level oversight of token compliance
